document.title: Nova Force — Series Bible
document.format: Structured bible (JSON). Expand into prose docs or pitch PDFs as needed.

lockStatus.notes: Promote sections from draft to locked when the team agrees — do not guess downstream.

pitch.logline: After a mysterious pulse awakens impossible abilities across Meridian City, five strangers who never asked to be heroes must become Nova Force — or watch their home fall to people who think 'safety' means owning them.
pitch.tone: Earnest teen ensemble drama with kinetic action and moral weight. Humor is a defense mechanism characters use when stakes get personal — not constant quips. The show trusts the audience with ambiguity: villains can believe they are right; heroes can be wrong in ways that hurt.
pitch.audience: Young adult / teen (roughly 13–24 primary), crossover for adults who want character-first superhero stories. Themes: responsibility vs performance, trust vs control, belonging vs surveillance.

comparableShows.title: Young Justice
comparableShows.why: Ensemble growth, conspiracy texture, consequences that stick.

comparableShows.title: Invincible (tone discipline, not gore)
comparableShows.why: Power has cost; institutions lie with confidence.

comparableShows.title: My Hero Academia
comparableShows.why: Clear power identity per character — Nova Force keeps powers narrower and more human-priced.

comparableShows.title: Spider-Man (any era)
comparableShows.why: Ordinary life pressure colliding with impossible responsibility.

whatNovaForceIsNot: A cynical parody of heroes — the world can joke, but the show does not sneer at hope.
A power-escalation arms race every episode — spikes matter because recovery does too.
A single chosen-one story — the team only wins when the roster complements, not when one character solos.

worldRules.powerOrigin: Powers in Season 1 are tied to the pulse event and its aftermath — activation is traumatic, uneven, and sometimes tied to prior exposure (experiments, stress, proximity). The full cosmology can deepen in later seasons; Season 1 only needs consistency, not every answer.
powerRules: Every ability has a visible or felt cost (fatigue, pain, sensory overload, emotional bleed) — see each hero's powersSpec in characters/*.json.
No one is omniscient; no one is globally untouchable. If a fight looks easy, you are probably missing the price.
Technology can blunt, redirect, or exploit powers — especially surveillance, insulation, jamming, and social engineering.
Powers do not replace personality: fear, pride, love, and shame still drive choices.
worldRules.publicKnowledge: After the pulse, Meridian knows 'something happened.' Viral clips, rumors, and conflicting official statements create a fog: some believe terrorism, some believe a solar flare, some believe the first supers. By mid-season, public opinion is a character — fear, fascination, and influencers turning kids into content.
worldRules.techLevel: Near-modern plus: smartphones, drones, dense CCTV, AR overlays, and aggressive data brokers. The show treats hacking and leaks as realistic weapons — speed matters, but so do air gaps and human error.
worldRules.lawEnforcementVsHeroes: Police are not monolithic. Some officers want help; others see unlicensed powers as a riot waiting to happen. There is no official 'hero license' in Season 1 — acting in public creates legal exposure, viral exposure, or both. The team is not above the law narratively; they navigate it messily.
worldRules.heroesLegalStatus: Nova Force is a team name the city learns — not a registered agency at start. Consequences of vigilantism, property damage, and injury are story fuel, not hand-waved.

shadowOrganization.workingNameStatus: draft — rename when a final title is chosen
shadowOrganization.philosophyInOneSentence: Freedom without oversight is extinction — they believe management is mercy.
shadowOrganization.details: See production/antagonist-bible.json → organization, theArchitect, episodicThreatRoster, henchmenAndSquadTemplates

meridianCity.overview: Meridian is a coastal American city big enough to have a skyline, a harbor, inequality, and neighborhoods that feel like different worlds. It is not Gotham-gray by default — sunlight is a storytelling tool — but danger lives in alleys, boardrooms, and feeds as much as in warehouses.

districts.name: Glasshook Pier
districts.vibe: Tourism, festivals, influencers — public chaos where a pulse event becomes instant content.

districts.name: Ironclad
districts.vibe: Industrial docks and night shifts — quiet violence, smuggling texture, good for chases that feel grounded.

districts.name: Verdance Heights
districts.vibe: Hills, older trees, wealth with walls — isolation, surveillance, and 'help' that arrives with strings.

districts.name: Lowline
districts.vibe: Dense apartments, bodegas, community networks — where Elias's quiet work and Priya's messy genius feel native.

districts.name: Meridian Central
districts.vibe: Downtown glass, protests, press packs, police cordons — where Titan's viral moment hits hardest.

landmarks: Meridian Spire — skyline anchor; good for wide shots and 'the city watches' moments.
Harbor Bridge — wind, height, stakes; finale-adjacent energy without locking a specific ending beat here.
Old Rail Yards — maze geometry for stealth and Wraith-friendly shadows.
meridianCity.teamLocationsStatus: draft — exact safehouse addresses and HQ logic should be locked during scripts. Principles: Glitch needs network redundancy; Titan needs load-bearing quiet; Wraith needs exits; Solace needs green space; Surge needs people nearby (whether he admits it or not).
meridianCity.publicLife: Meridian runs on coffee lines, transit delays, school gossip, and local sports hate — heroes breathe the same air. Social media is a chorus: memes, misinformation, and moral panic.

visualLanguage.reference: show.json style field: fusion of DC and Marvel animation — meaning cinematic posing with human micro-performance, not one flat house style.
visualLanguage.frameRate: 24 fps for cinematic animation passes unless pipeline demands otherwise — document exceptions in the style guide.
visualLanguage.resolutionTargets: Master at production resolution appropriate to AI pipeline; deliver 1080p minimum for web; archive higher if available.

heroColorKeys.usage: High-energy highlights; warmth vs danger in arcs — electricity reads tactile, imperfect.
Negative space and silhouette; crimson as warning, not romance default.
Weight and strain visible in light — green as stress under skin, not sparkle.
UI clutter and emotional tempo — color shifts track mental load, not fashion only.
Warmth and living texture — healing reads organic, never sterile.

visualLanguage.pacingNotes: Action breathes: let a face land before a city explodes. Episode rhythm follows Season 1 acts — intros (arcs 1–4), fracture (arcs 5–9), reckoning (arcs 10–12) — see Season 1/season.json.
aiAnimationNotes: Lock a style guide before scaling episode count — prompts, banned looks, and reference boards belong in a separate visual pipeline doc (NEXT_STEPS §3).
Micro-performance beats spectacle-only shots: hands, eyes, breath — this is what makes AI-assisted work feel authored.
Avoid 'plastic skin' defaults; keep asymmetry, imperfect light, and consequence in costumes (wear, tear, sweat).
